FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data: N=11,868 participants in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study followed from ages ~9/10 to ~13/14 across 21 United States sites.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone and social media variables: self-reported hours per day of social media (named platforms: Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, Snapchat, TikTok), texting on cell phone, video chat, YouTube, TV/Movies, and video games. All qualify as specific phone/SM variables.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multilevel logistic regression examining associations between technology subtypes (social media, texting, video chat, YouTube, TV, video games) and sociodemographic outcomes (biological sex, parent education, income, marital status). Inferential tests reported with statistics throughout. Example: 'increases in the subject-specific odds of using a technology >2 hr/day ranged from 25% (YouTube; 95% CI: 1.16–1.35) to 234% (social media; 95% CI: 3.14–3.55).'

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple ORs with 95% CIs reported in Table 2: social media OR 3.34 (95% CI: 3.14, 3.55), TV OR 2.16 (95% CI: 2.09, 2.23), video games OR 2.11 (95% CI: 2.04, 2.18), texting OR 2.22 (95% CI: 2.07, 2.39), video chat OR 1.79 (95% CI: 1.67, 1.93), YouTube OR 1.25 (95% CI: 1.16, 1.35).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using multilevel logistic regression to examine participant-specific changes and between-subject heterogeneity in probability of technology use >2 hours per day across 5 waves of data (ages 9/10 to 13/14).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed journal article in Journal of Adolescent Health, 2024. Not a preprint, conference abstract, or duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes BOTH cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses with smartphone/SM as dependent variable and demographic-only predictors. Per binding rule: FT-EC7 does NOT apply when paper also reports cross-sectional analyses with smartphone/SM as DV. Cross-sectional patterns documented at each wave (ages 9/10, 10/11, 11/12, 12/13, 13/14) satisfy cross-sectional criterion. Paper should be INCLUDED.
notes: C1 incorrectly marked FT_EC7_applies=true, missing that cross-sectional analyses are present alongside longitudinal models. C2 correctly identified FT_EC7 does not apply. Resolver agrees with C2. Paper is novel descriptive epidemiology of technology use patterns across subtypes and sociodemographics; includes both cross-sectional and longitudinal statistical associations.
